    Solution

    Write Great PRDs With This Skill

    Commits
    05.02.01

    Start here - added the /write-a-prd skill to the repo

    05.02.02

    See the solution - added my PRD to the repo

    Right now you know how to explore a problem space with a grill-me session. But what happens when the feature is too big to implement in one go?

    That's where the /to-prd skill comes in. It takes everything you've learned during your exploration and transforms it into a Product Requirements Document, a structured guide that captures your shared understanding.

    You don't have to write the PRD yourself. The skill does it for you, creating a document that's meaty enough to guide implementation later.

    Understanding the PRD Template

    The PRD template has a few key sections:

    SectionPurpose
    Problem StatementThe user's perspective on what's broken or missing
    SolutionFrom the user's perspective, what needs to happen
    User StoriesA numbered list of specific scenarios and outcomes

    This structure gives anyone picking up the PRD enough context to understand not just what to build, but why.

    Your Task: Build an Instructor Analytics Dashboard

    You're going to practice the full workflow. The instructor dashboard currently only shows learning progress tracking. That's limiting.

    What instructors actually need:

    • See how many users are enrolled in their courses
    • Track how much they're earning
    • View completion rates and quiz score distributions
    • Identify which lessons cause students to drop off

    This feature is huge and could grow in many directions. That makes it perfect for the grill-me plus PRD workflow.

    Steps To Complete

    Start Your Grill-Me Session

    • Run the /grill-me command to begin exploring the instructor analytics feature

    Ask questions until you reach a shared understanding of what this feature should include. You're aiming for clarity on scope, priorities, and user needs.

    Generate the PRD

    • Call the /to-prd skill instead of proceeding to implementation

    This converts your conversation into a structured PRD document that gets written locally to your codebase.

    Review and Reflect

    • Read through the generated PRD

    As you review it, consider:

    • Does this PRD accurately capture what you discussed?

    • Are the user stories clear enough for someone unfamiliar with the conversation?

    • What details feel missing or unclear?

    • If you were designing the PRD template, what would you add or change?

    • Jot down your observations about the process and the template itself

    What worked well? What felt awkward? These notes will help you refine the template over time.

    Commit Your Work

    • Do not implement the PRD yet

    Just save and commit the PRD document. The point of this exercise is to practice the discovery and documentation process, not to build the feature.

    The implementation comes later, once you're confident the PRD captures exactly what you want to build.
